title: Titus 1
subtitle: Appointing Elders and Opposing False Teachers

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — "Paul, a Servant of God and an Apostle of Jesus Christ"

heb.paragraph: Though the word does not appear in v. 3, the concept of divine manifestation (ἐπιφαίνω, 'to make visible,' related to ἐπιφάνεια) pervades the Pastorals' theology. In v. 3, God 'brought his word to light' (ἐφανέρωσεν) through preaching—the same revelation-language that in 2:11 and 3:4 describes grace and kindness 'appearing.' The Pastorals use this vocabulary to bridge eternal purpose and historical proclamation.

As in 1 Tim 1:1, Paul frames his apostleship as the result of divine command (ἐπιταγή, epitagē), not personal initiative or ecclesiastical appointment. The phrase 'God our Savior' (θεοῦ σωτῆρος) reappears as a Pastoral hallmark, simultaneously affirming Jewish monotheism and countering imperial soteriology.

panels.ctx: The salutation of Titus is the longest and most theologically dense of the three Pastoral openings. It compresses the entire Pastoral soteriology into three verses: God's eternal purpose (v. 2, 'before the beginning of time'), historical revelation through preaching (v. 3), and personal appropriation through faith (v. 4). Titus, like Timothy, is called a 'true son in our common faith'—establishing his delegated authority. Crete, where Titus was left to complete unfinished organizational work, was proverbially notorious for dishonesty (cf. v. 12, the 'Cretan paradox').

cross.note: The parallel creedal fragment in 2 Timothy also moves from pre-temporal purpose through historical appearing, confirming a shared liturgical tradition across the Pastoral Epistles.
The parallel salutation to Timothy uses nearly identical language (apostle by command, true son in faith), establishing a consistent pattern of delegated apostolic authority.

notes.note: Paul identifies himself as both servant and apostle—he serves God by exercising apostolic authority. The gospel rests on God's promise made before the beginning of time, now brought to light through preaching entrusted to Paul.

Titus is Paul's true son in their common faith. Grace and peace come from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior. The familial language establishes Titus's legitimacy as Paul's representative on Crete.

Paul's salutation packs a complete soteriology into four verses: God's eternal purpose, the revelation of the gospel at the appointed time, and its reception through faith. Ministers must remember that they serve a plan older than creation itself. Titus, as Paul's delegate, carries this eternal authority into the messy reality of Cretan church life.

The phrase 'before the beginning of time' (πρὸ χρόνων αἰωνίων) is identical to 2 Tim 1:9 and locates God's saving purpose in eternity, prior to creation—a claim with significant implications for the theology of election.
The phrase 'our common faith' (κοινὴν πίστιν) emphasizes the universal, shared character of the faith—against any suggestion that Paul and Titus hold to different versions of the gospel.

The salutation is the most compressed theological statement in the Pastorals: eternal purpose (πρὸ χρόνων αἰωνίων), historical revelation (ἐφανέρωσεν καιροῖς ἰδίοις), and apostolic commission (κατ’ ἐπιταγήν) form the theological backbone of the letter. The κοινή πίστις (common faith) binds Paul and Titus in a shared apostolic tradition that Titus must now implement on Crete.

The opening is simultaneously the letter's theological thesis and Titus's credentials statement. By tracing his commission from God's eternal promise through historical revelation to apostolic entrustment, Paul locates Titus's authority within a salvation-historical framework that the Cretan opponents cannot match. The 'common faith' language underscores the universality of the gospel against any local or ethnic modification.

heb.paragraph: The shift from πρεσβυτέρους (elders, v. 5) to ἐπίσκοπον (overseer, v. 7) within the same qualification list is the clearest NT evidence that the two terms refer to the same office. The elder-overseer interchangeability attested here (as in Acts 20:17, 28) represents the earlier, non-hierarchical structure that preceded the later bishop-presbyter-deacon hierarchy developed by Ignatius of Antioch.

panels.ctx: Titus's task on Crete is organizational: he must appoint elders in 'every town'—suggesting multiple house churches scattered across the island. The qualification list overlaps significantly with 1 Tim 3:1–7 but is adapted to the Cretan context: the emphasis on self-control, sobriety, and not being given to drunkenness may address specific Cretan cultural traits (cf. the Epimenides quotation in v. 12). The requirement that the elder 'hold firmly to the trustworthy message as it has been taught' (v. 9) directly connects leadership qualifications to the anti-heresy agenda of the letter.

cross.note: The parallel overseer qualifications in 1 Timothy provide a complementary list; comparing the two reveals both shared requirements and context-specific emphases.
Paul and Barnabas appointed elders in every church on the first missionary journey—the same organizational pattern Titus is now implementing on Crete.
Peter's instructions to elders emphasize willing service, humility, and example-setting, complementing the character-based criteria here.

notes.note: Titus was left on Crete to appoint elders in every town. An elder must be blameless, faithful to his wife, with believing children who are not accused of being wild or disobedient.

As God's steward, the overseer must be blameless: not overbearing, not quick-tempered, not given to drunkenness, not violent, not pursuing dishonest gain. He must be hospitable, love what is good, be self-controlled, upright, holy, and disciplined. He must hold firmly to the trustworthy message so that he can encourage others by sound doctrine and refute those who oppose it.

Paul left Titus on Crete to complete the church's organizational structure by appointing qualified elders. The transition from 'elder' to 'overseer' within the same list proves these are the same office. The qualifications are overwhelmingly moral, and the capstone requirement—holding firmly to sound doctrine—connects personal character to theological competence.

The transition from πρεσβυτέρους (v. 5) to ἐπίσκοπον (v. 7) within the same list is the clearest evidence in the NT that 'elder' and 'overseer' denote the same office at this stage of church development.
The phrase 'trustworthy message as it has been taught' (πιστοῦ λόγου κατὰ τὴν διδαχήν) implies a fixed body of apostolic teaching that elders must preserve and transmit.

The elder-overseer equivalence is demonstrated by the syntactic flow: πρεσβυτέρους (v. 5) introduces the topic, and δεῖ γὰρ τὸν ἐπίσκοπον (v. 7, 'for the overseer must') continues the same list. The Cretan adaptations—emphasis on sobriety, self-control, hospitality—may address specific cultural challenges. The capstone qualification—ἀντεχόμενον (holding firmly) to the trustworthy word—makes doctrinal competence a leadership prerequisite, directly countering the false teachers.

The elder-appointment instruction combines organizational pragmatism with theological purpose. The 'every town' scope reveals a Cretan mission field with multiple communities in need of stable leadership. The elder/overseer interchangeability reflects an ecclesiology in which function determines title: these leaders oversee the community's faith and practice. The final qualification—ability to exhort in sound doctrine and refute opponents—bridges the church-order and anti-heresy sections of the letter.

heb.paragraph: Paul quotes the Cretan poet Epimenides (c. 600 BC), who reportedly said: 'Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.' The quotation creates the famous 'Epimenides paradox' (also called the 'liar paradox'): if a Cretan says all Cretans are liars, is the statement true or false? Paul endorses the quotation's moral assessment (ἡ μαρτυρία αὕτη ἐστὶν ἀληθής, 'this testimony is true') and applies it to the false teachers on Crete, using their own cultural reputation against them.

panels.ctx: The false teachers on Crete are identified as belonging to 'the circumcision group' (v. 10)—Jewish Christians who combined Torah observance with speculative teaching. Their teaching of 'Jewish myths' (v. 14) and 'commands of those who reject the truth' suggests a blend of Jewish halakhic interpretation and Hellenistic religious speculation. The motivation is financial: they are 'ruining whole households for the sake of dishonest gain' (v. 11). Paul's use of the Epimenides quotation is a rhetorical strategy: citing a pagan poet respected by the Cretans to validate his critique.

cross.note: The parallel description of the Ephesian false teachers—devoted to myths, genealogies, and law-teaching—confirms similar false teaching across multiple Pauline churches.
Paul's citation of the Greek poet Aratus ('we are his offspring') in his Athens speech demonstrates his willingness to use pagan literature for apologetic purposes.
The 'circumcision group' causing trouble in Crete echoes the Judaizing opponents Paul confronted in Galatia and Antioch.

notes.note: There are many rebellious people, full of meaningless talk and deception, especially those of the circumcision group. They must be silenced because they are disrupting whole households by teaching things they ought not to teach—and that for the sake of dishonest gain.

Even one of their own prophets said, 'Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.' This testimony is true. Therefore rebuke them sharply, so that they will be sound in the faith and pay no attention to Jewish myths.

To the pure, all things are pure, but to those who are corrupted and do not believe, nothing is pure. They claim to know God, but by their actions they deny him. They are detestable, disobedient, and unfit for doing anything good.

The false teachers combine Jewish speculation with Cretan cultural vices—a toxic blend of bad theology and bad character. Paul's quotation of their own poet turns their cultural identity against them. The principle that 'to the pure, all things are pure' demolishes the opponents' ritual purity obsessions: defilement comes from the heart, not from food or external observances. Those who profess to know God but deny him by their conduct are the most dangerous of all hypocrites.

The quotation is attributed to Epimenides of Knossos (c. 600 BC), a semi-legendary Cretan prophet. Paul calls him a 'prophet' (προφήτης)—a term used loosely for any respected cultural figure, not necessarily implying divine inspiration.
The purity principle ('to the pure, all things are pure') echoes Rom 14:14, 20 and Mark 7:15. It directly counters the opponents' ritual purity requirements.

The identification of the opponents as οἱ ἐκ τῆς περιτομῆς (those of the circumcision) locates the heresy within Jewish Christianity. The Epimenides citation is rhetorically brilliant: Paul uses the opponents' own cultural tradition to discredit them. The financial motive (αἰσχροῦ κέρδους, shameful gain) echoes 1 Tim 6:5 and links heresy to avarice.

The sharp rebuke (ἀποτόμως, severely) aims at restoration (ἵνα ὑγιαίνωσιν, that they may be healthy in faith)—discipline serves therapy. The purity principle (v. 15) is the Pastorals' most concise statement against ritual legalism: purity is a quality of the person, not of the object. The devastating verdict in v. 16—knowing God by profession but denying him by practice—is the ultimate diagnosis of the opponents.

The anti-heresy section employs three rhetorical strategies: ethnic stereotype (the Epimenides quotation), financial exposal (dishonest gain), and theological diagnosis (professing but denying). Together they construct a comprehensive case for the opponents' illegitimacy. The purity declaration (v. 15) is particularly important: it rejects the ritual-purity framework that the circumcision group was imposing and replaces it with an internalized ethic consistent with Pauline freedom from Torah observance. The closing indictment—‘by their works they deny him'—is the Pastorals' consistent standard: authentic faith is demonstrated by conduct, not by claims to knowledge.

themes.note: Holiness dominates through the elder qualifications and the purity principle. Judgment is prominent in the confrontation with false teachers. Sovereignty and Faith undergird the eternal-purpose salutation.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: Salutation: eternal purpose and apostolic commission

rows.label: vv.10–16
rows.text: Cretan false teachers and sharp rebuke

lit.note: Chapter 1 moves from theological foundations (vv. 1–4) through organizational instruction (vv. 5–9) to polemical confrontation (vv. 10–16). The elder-appointment section bridges the two: qualified leaders are the antidote to false teaching.

hebtext.note: Though the word does not appear in v. 3, the concept of divine manifestation (ἐπιφαίνω, 'to make visible,' related to ἐπιφάνεια) pervades the Pastorals' theology. The elder-overseer interchangeability attested here (as in Acts 20:17, 28) represents the earlier, non-hierarchical structure that preceded the later bishop-presbyter-deacon hierarchy developed by Ignatius of Antioch.

Paul quotes the Cretan poet Epimenides (c. 600 BC), who reportedly said: 'Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.' The quotation creates the famous 'Epimenides paradox' (also called the 'liar paradox'): if a Cretan says all Cretans are liars, is the statement true or false? Paul endorses the quotation's moral assessment (ἡ μαρτυρία αὕτη ἐστὶν ἀληθής, 'this testimony is true') and applies it to the false teachers on Crete, using their own cultural reputation against them.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Titus
ppl.text: The salutation of Titus is the longest and most theologically dense of the three Pastoral openings…

ppl.name: Christ Jesus
ppl.text: Grace and peace come from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Appointing Elders and Opposing False Teachers

translations.text: Rather, he must be hospitable, one who loves what is good, who is self-controlled, upright, holy and disciplined.
but hospitable, a lover of good, self-controlled, upright, holy, and disciplined.
Since an overseer manages God’s household, he must be blameless—not overbearing, not quick-tempered, not given to drunkenness, not violent, not pursuing dishonest gain.
For an overseer, as God’s steward, must be above reproach. He must not be arrogant or quick-tempered or a drunkard or violent or greedy for gain,
